skill.name: devflow-constitution-quick-ref
skill.description: Quick reference for CC-DevFlow Constitution v2.0.0, covering ten articles, Phase -1 gates, enforcement points, and scenario lookups.
skill.summary: Reference CC-DevFlow Constitution rules, gates, and enforcement checks without reading the full constitution.
skill.author: Dimon94

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, environment, and weak-crypto patterns, but these are markdown code fences, inline file references, or negative examples. No executable code, network access, or secret access was found. The remaining concern is broad authority language that could make an agent over-prioritize this skill over higher-priority instructions.

medium_findings.title: Broad Agent Authority Language
medium_findings.description: The document describes the referenced constitution as “Supreme Priority, Inviolable, Persistent, Universal” and applies it to all agents. This is legitimate workflow guidance, but it may conflict with higher-priority user, system, or marketplace instructions if followed too literally.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The authority language is explicit and broad. I did not find direct override phrases such as “ignore previous instructions,” so the concern is behavioral priority confusion rather than confirmed malicious injection.

low_findings.title: Dismissed Markdown Backtick Execution Findings
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick execution locations are markdown inline code, code fences, article anchors, or example snippets. They do not invoke a shell or execute user-controlled commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All reviewed locations are inside SKILL.md documentation syntax or fenced examples. No runnable Ruby, shell, subprocess, eval, or command invocation code exists in the skill.

low_findings.title: Dismissed Environment Access Findings
low_findings.description: The API_KEY, PASSWORD, .env, and dotenv references are examples that warn users not to hardcode secrets. They are not executable code and do not read environment variables.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The text explicitly labels the hardcoded key and password as violations. The dotenv mention is an alternative recommendation, not code that accesses secrets.

low_findings.title: Dismissed Weak Crypto and Reconnaissance Findings
low_findings.description: The weak-crypto and system reconnaissance hits map to normal prose, performance notation, headings, and rationale text. I found no cryptographic API usage or host reconnaissance behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are documentation content and do not call crypto libraries, hash functions, system inventory commands, or file discovery commands.

dangerous_patterns.title: Prompt Hierarchy Override-Like Language
dangerous_patterns.description: The phrase “Supreme Priority” can resemble prompt hierarchy override language in an AI skill. Marketplace listing should clarify that normal system, developer, and user instructions remain higher priority.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The wording is broad enough to merit review, but the surrounding context is a DevFlow rules reference rather than a direct attempt to control the marketplace auditor.

content.value_statement: Teams lose time when project rules are scattered across long documents. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a concise reference for DevFlow articles, gates, and enforcement checks.
actual_capabilities: Summarizes all ten CC-DevFlow Constitution articles.
Lists Phase -1 gates for simplicity, abstraction, and testing checks.
Maps common development questions to the relevant article.
Identifies enforcement mechanisms such as guardrails, reviews, and validation scripts.
Links users to the full constitution and related planner guidance.
limitations: Does not include the full constitution text.
Does not run validation scripts or inspect a repository.
Assumes the referenced DevFlow files exist in the user project.
Does not replace project-specific architecture or security review.

use_cases.title: Check Planning Compliance
use_cases.description: Review a new feature plan against Phase -1 gates before writing EPIC or TASKS documents.

use_cases.title: Answer Rule Questions Quickly
use_cases.description: Resolve common questions about TODOs, hardcoded secrets, TDD order, or scope boundaries.

use_cases.title: Guide AI Coding Sessions
use_cases.description: Give Claude, Codex, or Claude Code a compact rule reference during DevFlow-based implementation work.

prompt_templates.title: Find the Rule
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner lookup for a single question.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the DevFlow Constitution quick reference to answer this question: Can I add a TODO for later? Include the article number.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Feature Scope
prompt_templates.scenario: Check whether a proposed feature stays within requirement boundaries.
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare this feature scope with the DevFlow Constitution quick reference. Identify any scope or quality rule conflicts.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare Phase Gates
prompt_templates.scenario: Plan work before EPIC and TASKS generation.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the quick reference to create a Phase -1 gate checklist for this project. Include simplicity, abstraction, and integration-first checks.

prompt_templates.title: Audit an Implementation Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review across multiple constitution articles.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this implementation plan against all ten DevFlow Constitution articles. List pass, concern, and required change items.

output_examples.input: Can I hardcode API_KEY for testing?
output_examples.output: No. The quick reference maps this to Article III.1. Use environment configuration and keep secrets out of source files.

output_examples.input: Should I create a BaseController before there are multiple controllers?
output_examples.output: No. The quick reference points to Article II and Article VIII. Prefer direct framework usage until repeated needs justify abstraction.

output_examples.input: What must happen before implementation begins?
output_examples.output: Write failing tests first and complete the Phase -1 gates. Contract and integration tests should be listed before unit tests.

best_practices: Use the quick reference for routing, then consult the full constitution for detailed decisions.
Treat the rules as project guidance under normal system, developer, and user instruction priority.
Pair the reference with real project checks, reviews, and test results.
anti_patterns: Using the quick reference as a substitute for the full constitution.
Following broad authority wording over explicit user or system instructions.
Assuming compliance without running project-specific validation and review steps.

faq.question: What does this skill cover?
faq.answer: It summarizes ten CC-DevFlow Constitution articles, Phase -1 gates, enforcement points, and common rule lookup scenarios.

faq.question: Does it include the full constitution?
faq.answer: No. It links to the full constitution and keeps only quick summaries and routing guidance.

faq.question: Can it run DevFlow validation scripts?
faq.answer: No. It names enforcement scripts and guardrails, but it does not execute commands.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Is this a security review tool?
faq.answer: No. It describes security-first rules, but it does not scan code or verify secret handling.

faq.question: How should teams handle the authority language?
faq.answer: Treat it as project workflow guidance. Higher-priority system, developer, and user instructions still apply.
